title.en: Multi-Head Attention: Many Perspectives at Once

description.en: One attention computation can only attend one way at a time. Transformers run several attention 'heads' in parallel — each with its own Q/K/V projections — then concatenate their outputs and mix them with a final linear layer. Learn why multiple heads matter, the exact split/concat mechanics, and why the cost stays close to a single big head.

story_variants.en:
## One Head Can Only Look One Way

In the previous lesson you built scaled dot-product attention: a token forms a **query**, compares it against every **key**, and uses the resulting weights to take a weighted sum of **values**. It works — but it has a quiet limitation. A single attention computation produces *one* set of attention weights per token. That means it can emphasize *one* kind of relationship at a time.

Real language needs several at once. Consider the sentence *"The dog that chased the cat was tired."* To understand `was`, the model must (a) track grammar to find its subject `dog`, (b) reach across the long relative clause `that chased the cat`, and (c) keep `dog` and `cat` distinct so it does not attach `was` to the wrong noun. One head, forced to average all of these signals into a single weight pattern, does each of them poorly.

> **Key idea:** Multi-head attention runs *h* independent attention computations in parallel, each free to specialize in a different kind of relationship, then merges their answers. More perspectives, almost the same cost.

## Split, Attend, Concat, Project

The mechanics are precise and worth holding in your head as four steps. Suppose the model dimension is `d_model = 512` and we want `h = 8` heads. We choose a per-head dimension `d_k = d_model / h = 64`.

1. **Project into Q, K, V.** Each token's embedding is linearly projected to queries, keys, and values. In practice the model learns separate weight matrices per head — equivalently, it produces full `d_model` Q/K/V vectors and **splits** each into `h` slices of size `d_k`. Head 0 gets dimensions `0..63`, head 1 gets `64..127`, and so on.
2. **Attend, per head.** Each head runs the *same* scaled dot-product attention you already know, but only on its own 64-dim slice. Head 1 might learn to track the subject; head 5 might learn coreference; head 7 might watch the immediately preceding token. Crucially, the scaling factor is now `sqrt(d_k)`, not `sqrt(d_model)`.
3. **Concatenate.** Each head outputs a `d_k`-dimensional vector per token. Lay the `h` head-outputs back-to-back **in head order** to rebuild a single `d_model`-dimensional vector: `[head0 | head1 | ... | head7]`. This concatenation is pure bookkeeping — no math, just placing slices in the right positions.
4. **Final linear mix.** A learned output matrix `W_O` (shape `d_model x d_model`) mixes the concatenated heads so information discovered by one head can influence the others. Without this step the heads would never talk.

## Why It Costs About the Same

A tempting worry: eight heads must cost eight times more. It does not. Each head works in `d_k = d_model / h` dimensions, so the attention math per head is roughly `1/h` of a full-dimension computation. Multiply by `h` heads and you are back near the cost of a *single* full-width attention — plus the one extra `W_O` projection.

| Aspect | Single head (d=512) | 8 heads (d_k=64 each) |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Attention patterns per token | 1 | 8 (one per head) |
| Dim each head works in | 512 | 64 |
| Relationships captured | one blended signal | up to 8 specialized signals |
| Total attention compute | baseline | ~same as baseline |
| Extra parameters | — | one output matrix W_O |

So you pay almost nothing in compute and gain a large jump in **representational power**: the model can attend to syntax, long-range dependencies, and coreference *simultaneously* instead of smearing them together.

## The Part You Will Build

The learnable projections (Q/K/V and W_O) are trained by gradient descent — not something you hand-code in this lesson. But the **split** and **concat** steps are pure, deterministic plumbing, and getting their *ordering* right is what makes multi-head attention actually work. Swap two head slices and the final `W_O` mixes garbage. In the challenge you will implement that bookkeeping yourself: take a list of per-head output vectors and concatenate them, in order, into the combined multi-head output. Master this and the rest of multi-head attention is just the dot-product attention you already know, run eight times in parallel.

nodes.label: Multi-Head Attention
children: 1. Split: d_model -> h heads of d_k
3. Concat: lay head outputs back-to-back (in order)
4. Project: mix with output matrix W_O

left.label: Single head (d=512): one blended attention pattern

right.label: 8 heads (d_k=64): syntax + long-range + coreference at once

description.en: Same total compute, far more representational power. Each head specializes; W_O lets them share what they found.

animation_sequence.code:
def split_heads(vec, h):
    d_k = len(vec) // h
    return [vec[i*d_k:(i+1)*d_k] for i in range(h)]

def concat_heads(heads):
    out = []
    for head in heads:
        out.extend(head)
    return out

v = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
heads = split_heads(v, 3)
print(heads)
print(concat_heads(heads))

executionSteps.description: A 6-dim Q/K/V vector to split across 3 heads
split_heads: each head gets a contiguous 2-dim slice, in order
Head 0 = dims 0..1, head 1 = dims 2..3, head 2 = dims 4..5
concat_heads rebuilds the full vector — split and concat are inverses

output: h heads run dot-product attention in PARALLEL.
Each works in d_k = d_model / h dims, so total cost ~ one big head.
Concat in head ORDER, then mix with W_O. Order matters.

instruction.en: You are building the split/concat plumbing of multi-head attention. Implement two pure-Python functions. concat_heads(heads): given a list of per-head output vectors (lists of numbers), concatenate them back-to-back IN ORDER into one combined vector. split_heads(vec, h): given a flat vector and a head count h, split it into h contiguous, equal-size slices in order (the inverse of concat). No numpy — just lists. With the demo data, concat_heads([[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]]) must give [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6] and split_heads([10, 20, 30, 40], 2) must give [[10, 20], [30, 40]]. Print the two results on one line. Expected output: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6] [[10, 20], [30, 40]]

practice_challenges.starter_code:
def concat_heads(heads):
    # TODO: lay each head's vector back-to-back, in order, into one flat list
    return None

def split_heads(vec, h):
    # TODO: split vec into h contiguous equal-size slices, in order
    # (this is the inverse of concat_heads)
    return None

# --- demo (do not change) ---
combined = concat_heads([[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]])
heads = split_heads([10, 20, 30, 40], 2)
print(combined, heads)

en: concat_heads: start with an empty list and extend it with each head in turn, e.g. out.extend(head). Order is just the order they appear in the input list.
split_heads: the per-head size is d_k = len(vec) // h. Head i covers indices i*d_k up to (i+1)*d_k.
A clean split: [vec[i*d_k:(i+1)*d_k] for i in range(h)] where d_k = len(vec)//h.
Sanity check the inverse property: split_heads(concat_heads(x), len(x)) should return x.

q.en: What is the main reason transformers use multiple attention heads instead of one?

en: It makes the model use less memory
Each head can specialize in a different kind of relationship (syntax, long-range, coreference) instead of blending them into one pattern
It removes the need for the softmax
A single head cannot run on a GPU

q.en: With d_model = 512 and h = 8 heads, what dimension does each head work in?

en: 512, the full model dimension
8, the number of heads
64, because d_k = d_model / h = 512 / 8
4096, because the dimensions are multiplied

q.en: After the h heads each produce their output, what are the next two steps?

en: Average the heads, then apply softmax
Concatenate the head outputs in order, then mix them with a learned output matrix W_O
Drop the smallest head, then normalize
Add the heads together, then split again

q.en: Why doesn't using 8 heads cost roughly 8x the compute of a single head?

en: Because heads share the same weights
Because each head works in d_model/h dimensions, so 8 heads of 1/8 the size sum to about one full-width attention
Because the heads run on separate machines
Because softmax is skipped for extra heads
